name: Dal Makhani
description: Slow-cooked whole black lentils and kidney beans simmered overnight in butter and cream — the crown jewel of Punjabi dhabas.
instructions: Pressure cook soaked dal and kidney beans with salt for 8-10 whistles until very soft.
Mash some lentils against the pot to thicken — this is key.
In a heavy pan, fry cumin seeds and bay leaf in butter until aromatic.
Add onion and cook 20 minutes until deep golden-brown.
Add garlic, ginger paste and kashmiri chili; cook 5 minutes.
Pour in tomato puree; bhunao (stir-fry) until oil separates — 15 minutes minimum.
Add cooked dal to the masala; simmer on the lowest heat for at least 2 hours, stirring occasionally.
Stir in cream and garam masala; simmer 15 more minutes.
Finish with a generous knob of cold butter for sheen.
tips: The longer it simmers, the better it gets. Authentic dhabas cook it for 8-12 hours. A charcoal dum at the end gives the signature smoky flavor.

name: Rajma Masala
description: Red kidney beans slow-cooked in a robust Punjabi tomato-onion masala — the ultimate comfort food served over steaming rice.
instructions: Fry cumin seeds until they pop, then add onions.
Cook onion on medium 20-25 minutes until very deeply browned — this builds the entire flavor base.
Add garlic and ginger paste; cook 3 minutes.
Add tomatoes and all spices except garam masala; bhunao until oil separates completely.
Add kidney beans with a cup of their liquid (or water).
Simmer 25-30 minutes, mashing a few beans to thicken.
Add garam masala; simmer 5 more minutes.
Garnish with cilantro and serve with steamed basmati rice.
tips: The deeply browned onion is non-negotiable — most home cooks under-cook it. Go until it's nearly black at the edges.

name: Hyderabadi Dum Biryani
description: The royal kachchi style — raw marinated chicken layered with half-cooked fragrant rice and sealed for slow dum cooking. A true labor of love.

ingredient_quantities.whole spices: 2 bay leaves, 4 cardamom, 2 star anise, 1 cinnamon stick

instructions: Marinate chicken in yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, lemon juice, half the fried onions, mint, cilantro, and chilies for minimum 4 hours.
Half-cook rice in heavily salted boiling water with whole spices — exactly 70% done. Drain.
Steep saffron in warm milk.
Layer raw marinated chicken at the bottom of a heavy pot.
Add half the rice, scatter mint, cilantro, and fried onions.
Add remaining rice; drizzle saffron milk and ghee over the top.
Seal the pot with dough or foil, then the lid. Cook on high 5 min, then lowest heat 35-40 min.
Rest sealed for 15 min before opening. Serve layered without mixing.
tips: Kachchi biryani uses raw meat — the steam from the meat cooks the rice. The seal (dum) is everything: no steam should escape.

name: Masala Dosa with Coconut Chutney
description: Crispy, golden South Indian rice-and-lentil crepe filled with spiced potato masala, served with coconut chutney and sambar.
instructions: Soak rice and urad dal separately for 6 hours; grind to a smooth, slightly grainy batter.
Mix together, add salt, and ferment at room temperature 12-16 hours until airy.
For the masala: temper mustard seeds, curry leaves, and chilies; add onion and cook until soft.
Add mashed potatoes, turmeric, and salt; mix and stuff.
For chutney: blend fresh coconut, ginger, green chili with water; temper with mustard seeds and curry leaves.
Heat a cast iron griddle; pour a ladle of batter and spread in a thin spiral.
Drizzle oil around edges; cook on medium until golden and crisp.
Place masala in center; fold dosa and serve immediately with chutney and sambar.
tips: The fermentation is everything — a 12-hour ferment in a warm place gives tang and crispness. Cold batter makes soft, pale dosas.

name: Kerala Meen Moilee (Fish Curry)
description: A delicate, golden Kerala fish curry in thin coconut milk with green chilies and turmeric — light, fragrant, and subtly spiced.
instructions: Heat coconut oil; temper mustard seeds until they pop.
Add curry leaves, onion, ginger, garlic, and green chilies; cook until onion is soft but not browned.
Add turmeric and black pepper; stir 1 minute.
Pour in thin coconut milk and tomatoes; simmer 5 minutes.
Gently add fish; cook 8-10 minutes without stirring much.
Pour thick coconut milk over the top; heat gently (do not boil).
Taste and adjust salt; serve with appam or steamed rice.
tips: Never boil after adding thick coconut milk — it will split. The moilee should be golden, not deep orange.

name: Kashmiri Rogan Josh
description: Tender lamb slow-braised in a Kashmiri-style sauce with whole aromatic spices and ratanjot for its iconic deep red color, no onion or tomato.

ingredient_quantities.whole spices: 4 cloves, 4 green cardamom, 2 black cardamom, 1 cinnamon stick, 2 bay leaves

instructions: Heat mustard oil to smoking point; reduce heat and add asafoetida until it sizzles.
Add whole spices; fry 30 seconds until aromatic.
Add lamb pieces; sear on high until well browned on all sides — 10-12 minutes.
Mix kashmiri chili in 2 tbsp water to make a paste; add and stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add whisked yogurt 2 tbsp at a time, stirring constantly between each addition.
Add fennel powder, ginger powder, and salt.
Add 2 cups hot water; bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer.
Cover and cook 60-90 minutes until lamb is fall-off-the-bone tender.
The oil should float on top — that is how you know it is ready.
tips: Authentic Kashmiri rogan josh uses no onion or tomato — its depth comes from yogurt, whole spices, and the bhunao (frying) technique.

name: Chole Bhature
description: Spiced, tangy Punjabi chickpeas served with pillowy deep-fried bhature — the beloved street breakfast of Delhi.
instructions: Pressure cook chickpeas with a tea bag (removed after) until very soft.
Blend onion to a paste; cook in oil until deep reddish-brown.
Add garlic-ginger paste; cook 3 minutes.
Add tomato puree and chole masala; bhunao 15 minutes until oil separates.
Add chickpeas with liquid; mash a few; simmer 20 minutes.
Finish with amchur for tartness.
For bhature: mix flour, yogurt, oil, salt, soda water into soft dough; rest 30 min.
Roll into ovals; deep fry in hot oil until puffed and golden.
Serve chole with hot bhature, raw onion rings, and green chutney.
tips: The tea bag trick gives chole their characteristic dark color without affecting flavor. Never skip the amchur — it is what makes them genuinely Punjabi.

name: Sarson da Saag with Makki di Roti
description: Slow-cooked Punjabi mustard greens with spinach and bathua, tempered in ghee, served with handmade cornmeal flatbread — pure winter soul food.
instructions: Pressure cook all greens with garlic, ginger, chilies, and a pinch of salt for 3 whistles.
Cool and blend coarsely — leave some texture.
In a heavy pan, fry onion in ghee until golden.
Add blended greens; stir in makki atta (2-3 tbsp) to thicken.
Simmer on low 30-40 minutes, stirring regularly. Add jaggery.
Finish with a large spoonful of white butter.
For roti: knead makki atta with hot water into a pliable dough.
Pat by hand (not rolled) into rounds; cook on a tawa both sides.
Serve saag with generous cold butter and makki roti.
tips: Makki atta (cornmeal) needs hot water and a gentle hand — it tears if handled too hard. True sarson saag always has some mustard bitterness.

name: Pav Bhaji
description: Mumbai's beloved street food — a fiery, buttery mixed vegetable mash served with soft butter-toasted bread rolls.
instructions: Boil and mash vegetables and potatoes together; set aside.
Melt 3 tbsp butter in a wide flat pan (tava); add garlic paste and cook 1 minute.
Add onion and capsicum; cook until soft.
Add tomatoes and kashmiri chili; cook until very mushy.
Add pav bhaji masala; stir and cook 2 minutes.
Add mashed vegetables; mix and bhunao on high 5-8 minutes, pressing and smearing.
Add more butter as you cook — do not be shy.
Finish with lemon juice and cilantro.
Toast pav in butter on the tava until golden.
Serve bhaji with finely chopped raw onion and lemon wedges.
tips: The bhunao (pressing and smearing) on a flat tava develops the char and depth. More butter equals more authentic Mumbai street flavor.

name: Shorshe Maach (Bengali Mustard Fish)
description: Hilsa or rohu fish cooked in a pungent mustard paste with green chilies and turmeric — the iconic fish dish of Bengal.
instructions: Soak mustard seeds in water 30 minutes; grind with 2 chilies and water into a smooth paste.
Marinate fish with turmeric and salt for 15 minutes.
Heat mustard oil to smoking; fry fish until golden on both sides; remove.
In same oil, add onion seeds until they splutter.
Add remaining slitted chilies; stir 30 seconds.
Add mustard paste; cook 2-3 minutes until the raw smell is gone.
Add 1 cup water; bring to a boil.
Slide fried fish in; cover and simmer 10 minutes.
Finish with a drizzle of raw mustard oil for authenticity.
tips: The raw mustard oil drizzle at the end is essential for the authentic bitter-pungent Bengali finish. Use only stone-ground mustard paste.

name: Kadhi Pakora
description: Soft chickpea-flour fritters suspended in a silky, tangy yogurt-based curry — a North Indian staple that tastes like a hug.
instructions: Whisk yogurt with 4 tbsp besan, turmeric, salt, and 3 cups water until smooth.
Cook this mixture on low heat 30-35 minutes, stirring constantly — it will thicken.
For pakoras: mix 1 cup besan with onion, spinach, cumin, and water into thick batter.
Deep fry spoonfuls until golden; drain and soak in warm water 5 minutes to soften.
Add soaked pakoras to the kadhi; simmer 10 minutes.
Prepare tadka: fry cumin, mustard, curry leaves, asafoetida, and dried chilies in oil until fragrant.
Pour sizzling tadka over the kadhi; add garam masala.
Serve over steamed rice.
tips: Sour yogurt is essential — sweet yogurt makes a flat-tasting kadhi. Soaking the fried pakoras in water before adding them keeps them soft.

name: Rasam
description: South India's vibrant, peppery tamarind and tomato broth — a digestive, a medicine, and a comfort in one thin, fiery cup.
instructions: Soak tamarind in 2 cups warm water 10 minutes; squeeze and strain the extract.
Cook toor dal until mushy; reserve.
Combine tamarind water, tomatoes, turmeric, and salt; boil 5 minutes.
Add cooked dal liquid (not the solid dal) for body.
Add crushed garlic, pepper, cumin; simmer 10 minutes.
In ghee, fry mustard seeds, curry leaves, asafoetida, and chilies; pour into rasam.
Bring just to a boil; remove from heat (don't over-cook).
Finish with fresh cilantro and serve as a soup or over rice.
tips: Rasam should never be thick — it is a broth. Add more tamarind water if needed. The pepper-cumin combo is the heart of the flavor.

name: Goan Prawn Curry
description: Tangy, spiced Goan prawns in a rich coconut milk and Konkani masala gravy with kodampuli (Goan kokum) for its distinctive tartness.
instructions: Dry roast kashmiri chilies, coriander seeds, cumin, and black pepper until fragrant.
Blend with dry coconut, ginger, garlic, and a little water into a smooth red paste.
Heat coconut oil; cook onion until golden.
Add tomatoes; cook until mushy.
Add the ground masala paste; cook 5-8 minutes on medium.
Pour in coconut milk; simmer 5 minutes.
Add kokum and prawns; cook 5-7 minutes until prawns are just done.
Adjust salt; serve with steamed rice or Goan pav.
tips: Kokum is the soul of Goan fish curries — it gives a fruity tartness unlike any other souring agent. Don't substitute with lemon juice if you can help it.

name: Kheer (Rice Pudding)
description: Slow-cooked Indian rice pudding in full-fat milk, perfumed with cardamom, saffron, and topped with pistachios and rose petals.
instructions: Bring milk to a boil in a heavy-bottomed pan; reduce heat.
Add drained rice; simmer on low, stirring frequently.
Cook 45-60 minutes until rice is completely broken down and milk has thickened.
Add sugar and crushed cardamom; stir until dissolved.
Steep saffron in 2 tbsp warm milk; add to kheer.
Simmer 15 more minutes until it coats the back of a spoon.
Remove from heat; add rose water.
Garnish with nuts and raisins; serve warm or chilled.
tips: Kheer thickens significantly as it cools. Remove from heat when it still looks a little thin. Low and slow is the only way — never rush the milk reduction.

name: Aloo Paratha
description: Whole wheat flatbread stuffed with a spiced mashed potato filling, cooked on a tawa with generous ghee — Punjab's greatest breakfast.
instructions: Make a soft dough with flour, water, salt, and a little oil; rest 20 minutes.
Mash potatoes completely — no lumps or they will pierce the dough.
Mix mashed potato with chilies, ginger, cilantro, cumin, amchur, garam masala, and salt.
Divide dough and filling into equal balls.
Roll dough into a 4-inch circle; place filling in center.
Gather edges and seal; press gently and roll into a 7-inch round.
Cook on a hot tawa; flip when brown spots appear.
Apply ghee generously both sides; press lightly while cooking.
Serve hot with white butter, yogurt, and mango pickle.
tips: The filling must be completely dry and smooth — moist filling causes the paratha to burst. Press the paratha while cooking for even browning.

name: Matar Paneer
description: Fresh Indian cottage cheese and sweet green peas in a fragrant tomato-onion gravy — one of the most beloved North Indian vegetarian dishes.
instructions: Blend onion with cashews; cook in oil until golden and fragrant.
Add garlic-ginger paste; cook 2 minutes.
Add pureed tomatoes and all spices except garam masala; bhunao until oil separates.
Add peas; cook 5 minutes.
Add paneer cubes (pan-fried or fresh); stir gently.
Add 1/2 cup water; simmer 10 minutes.
Stir in cream and garam masala; simmer 3 minutes.
Adjust seasoning and serve with roti or naan.
tips: Pan-frying paneer before adding gives it a golden crust that holds up in the gravy. Cashews in the onion paste add a subtle richness and help bind the sauce.

name: Chicken Tikka Masala
description: Charred tandoor-style chicken tikka in a rich, spiced tomato-cream sauce — a step above butter chicken with more pronounced smokiness and spice.
instructions: Marinate chicken with yogurt, 2 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, kashmiri chili, garam masala, oil, and salt — minimum 4 hours.
Grill or broil chicken on high until charred in spots; set aside.
In butter + oil, cook onion until golden.
Add remaining ginger-garlic paste; cook 3 minutes.
Add canned tomatoes, paprika, coriander, and cumin; simmer 15 minutes.
Blend the sauce smooth; return to pan.
Add cream, garam masala, and salt; simmer 10 minutes.
Add grilled chicken; simmer 10 more minutes.
Garnish with cream swirl and cilantro.
tips: The char on the tikka is non-negotiable — it gives the dish its depth. Don't discard the yogurt drippings; they go into the sauce.

name: Keema Matar
description: Spiced minced lamb or beef cooked with sweet green peas in a robust North Indian masala — quick, deeply savory, and endlessly satisfying.
instructions: Fry whole spices and cumin seeds until aromatic.
Add onion; cook until deep golden — 15 minutes.
Add garlic-ginger paste; cook 2 minutes.
Add minced meat; break up and brown on high heat until no pink remains.
Add tomatoes and all ground spices; bhunao until oil separates.
Add peas; cover and cook on low 15 minutes.
Finish with garam masala and fresh cilantro.
Serve with naan, paratha, or steamed rice.
tips: Browning the keema on high heat is critical — steaming it on low leaves it grey and bland. Cook off all the water first before adding the masala.

name: Aviyal
description: Kerala's royal mixed vegetable dish — an assortment of vegetables cooked with coconut, yogurt, and curry leaves in a light turmeric broth.
instructions: Cook harder vegetables (drumstick, yam, carrots) in salted water with turmeric until just tender.
Add softer vegetables (banana, beans) and raw mango; cook until all are done.
Grind coconut with green chilies and cumin into a coarse paste.
Stir coconut paste into vegetables; simmer 3-5 minutes.
Remove from heat; fold in yogurt.
Finish with coconut oil and curry leaves.
Serve as part of a Kerala sadya.
tips: Never cook aviyal after adding yogurt — the heat will curdle it. Use a variety of textures — some firm, some soft.

name: Gulab Jamun
description: Soft, spongy milk-solid dumplings fried golden and soaked in cardamom-rose sugar syrup — India's most beloved dessert.
instructions: Make syrup: dissolve sugar in water; add cardamom and saffron; simmer 5 minutes to one-string consistency. Add rose water; keep warm.
Mix milk powder, flour, baking soda with ghee.
Add cream gradually to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Oil your palms and roll into smooth balls — no cracks.
Deep fry on low heat (not hot) for 8-10 minutes, rolling constantly for even browning.
Remove when deep golden-brown; immediately drop into warm syrup.
Let soak minimum 2 hours — overnight is ideal.
Serve warm with a scoop of vanilla ice cream or rabri.
tips: Low oil temperature is the secret — too hot and the outside browns before the inside cooks. They should almost float and slowly color. Also, cracks in the dough mean the gulab jamun will break in oil.
